things.name: Reims Cathedral (UNESCO)
things.description: One of the greatest Gothic cathedrals in Europe, built from 1211; the traditional coronation church of French kings with 2,303 sculptures and Chagall windows

things.name: Palace of Tau (UNESCO)
things.description: UNESCO World Heritage former archbishop's palace next to the cathedral with the original statuary from the cathedral facade and royal coronation treasures

things.name: Champagne Cellars
things.description: The great Champagne houses Mumm, Taittinger, Ruinart and Pommery offer cellar tours through 10km of Roman chalk tunnels; an incomparable experience

things.name: Museum of Fine Arts
things.description: Impressive regional museum in a former abbey with outstanding collections of Flemish tapestries, Corot landscapes and works by Cranach and David

things.name: World War I Surrender Room (Musee de la Reddition)
things.description: In this schoolroom on 7 May 1945, German officers signed the unconditional surrender ending WWII in Europe; preserved exactly as it was that historic night

things.name: Saint-Remi Basilica (UNESCO)
things.description: UNESCO World Heritage 11th-century Romanesque basilica where St. Remigius baptised Clovis, king of the Franks; contains the saint's magnificent Gothic tomb

things.name: Place Drouet d'Erlon
things.description: Reims's lively pedestrian boulevard with fountains, cafes and the city's main outdoor space; the social heart of the city flanked by Art Deco and Belle Epoque facades

things.name: Mumm Champagne House
things.description: G.H. Mumm, Grand Prix winner's champagne; guided cellar tours and tastings of their prestige cuvees in the legendary chalk tunnels beneath Reims

things.name: Carnegie Library
things.description: Magnificent Art Deco library funded by Andrew Carnegie in 1928 with a striking mosaic facade; one of the finest examples of post-WWI Reims reconstruction

things.name: Stade Auguste-Delaune
things.description: Home of Stade de Reims with 21,684 capacity; one of France's great historic clubs, six-time champions of France and two-time European Cup finalists in the 1950s
